scenario: You've been attempting to produce a coal with a bow drill for 25 minutes. Both your spindle (willow) and fireboard (willow) are locally sourced but it's been raining for 2 days. You're seeing smoke from the notch but no sustained coal. What is the single most likely cause?

options.text: Insufficient downward pressure on the handhold
Wood moisture content is too high — both spindle and board are saturated
The notch angle is incorrect — it should be 1/8 of the depression
The bow speed is too slow for the wood species combination

explanation: Surface moisture on bow drill components is the #1 cause of failure in field conditions. Wood above ~15% moisture content will not sustain friction ignition — the steam produced as moisture evaporates cools the dust pile and prevents it from reaching 800°F+ ignition temperature. Solutions: split the wood to expose dry interior heartwood, keep components inside your clothing/sleeping bag overnight, or use a friction fire starter only after exposure to body heat for several hours. Willow is a good wood pairing — the issue here is moisture, not species selection. Notch should be 1/8 of the fire circle, and pressure/speed are secondary to dry wood.

scenario: You've collected dry tinder (cattail fluff), finger-size kindling (dry twigs), and wrist-size fuel wood (dead branches). You've selected a site. Place these steps in correct order for a safe, reliable fire build.

items.text: Clear a 3-foot radius of dead leaves, pine needles, and dry debris around the fire site
Build a tinder bundle and place it at center of cleared area
Build a small teepee of finger-sized kindling above and around the tinder — leave a 'door' opening on the windward side
Add 3-4 pieces of wrist-sized fuel in a loose teepee or log cabin around the kindling structure
Light the tinder bundle through the windward door opening using a match or spark at the base

explanation: Always clear the debris radius FIRST — most wildfires start from embers landing in surrounding material, not from the fire itself. Build from smallest (tinder) to largest — adding fuel before kindling catches is the most common beginner failure. The windward opening allows oxygen to feed the flame and gives your ignition source access to the base of the tinder. Light at the BASE — heat rises. Never light from the top. Once the kindling catches, add fuel gradually. Never leave a fire unattended, even for 30 seconds in dry conditions.

start.scenario: It's been raining for 3 days. Everything looks wet. You need fire to boil water for your group. You have: 2 matches, a ferrocerium rod, and a fixed-blade knife. No commercial tinder. What's your first action?

options.text: Search for dead standing wood (snags) and split it to expose dry heartwood
Look for birch trees or resinous pine — both have ignition-resistant bark and pitch

dead_standing.scenario: Good. A dead standing snag of hardwood will have dry interior even after days of rain. You find a 4-inch diameter dead oak snag. Do you:

options.text: Baton your knife through the center to expose heartwood, then shave fine feather sticks
Try to break it into pieces and use the interior chunks directly

feather_sticks_oak.explanation: Dead standing wood retains dry interior even in multi-day rain. Batoning to expose heartwood is the correct technique. Fine feather sticks from dry heartwood are excellent tinder. The ferro rod produces a hotter spark than matches — save matches for backup. This is the correct wet-weather sequence: find, split, shave, structure, ignite.

interior_chunks.explanation: Tinder must be FINE — the ignition temperature threshold depends on exposed surface area. A chunk of dry wood, no matter how dry, will not catch from a spark or match. You must shave or create feather sticks to expose fine fibers with high surface area. The interior dry wood was the right material — the wrong form.

resinous_sources.scenario: You find a standing dead pine. The base where dead branches were is packed with dried pitch (fatwood). Fatwood burns even when wet because the resin repels water and has a high BTU content. Do you:

options.text: Collect the pitch-saturated wood at branch junctions and the base, shave tinder from it
Use the birch bark you find on a fallen log nearby as your primary tinder

fatwood_tinder.explanation: Fatwood (resin-saturated pine heartwood) is the best wet-weather natural tinder. It ignites at lower temperatures due to turpentine content and burns at over 1,000°F. Always check pine snags and base junctions for yellow-orange resin-soaked wood. It smells strongly of pine resin. This is a top-tier wet fire starting skill.

birch_bark.explanation: Birch bark contains betulin and other oils that make it highly flammable even when damp. Both birch bark and fatwood are excellent wet-condition tinders. Birch bark ignites even when slightly wet due to oil content. Note: only use fallen/dead birch — peeling live birch bark kills the tree. The outer papery layers are the tinder; the inner layers are kindling material. Use whatever resinous or oil-containing material you can find in wet conditions.

scenario: You're setting up camp in a dry pine forest. Wind is from the northwest at about 10mph. You plan to build a fire. Which action is the HIGHEST PRIORITY safety step before lighting?

options.text: Build the fire in a depression at least 6 inches deep to contain embers
Clear all combustible material (dead leaves, pine needles, dry grass) in a minimum 3-foot radius around the fire site
Position the fire to the southeast of your shelter so smoke blows away from camp
Limit fire diameter to 18 inches maximum

explanation: Ember control is the #1 priority. The vast majority of wildfire ignitions from campfires happen when a single ember lands in surrounding dry material. The debris clearance radius is non-negotiable — 3 feet minimum in dry conditions, 5 feet in very dry/windy conditions. Building in a depression helps contain heat but doesn't stop airborne embers. Positioning relative to shelter matters for comfort but is not a safety priority. Limiting size is good practice but secondary to clearing the fuel bed. Scrape down to mineral soil in the fire area when possible.
